title: High-Throughput Generation of 3D Graphene Metamaterials and Property Quantification Using Machine Learning
abstract: 3D graphene assemblies are proposed as solutions to meet the goal toward efficient utilization of 2D graphene sheets, showing excellent performances in applications such as mechanical support, energy storage, and electrochemical catalysis. However, given the diversity and complexity of possible graphene 3D structures, there does not yet exist a systematic approach that can generate target 3D shapes and also, evaluate their performance. Here high-throughput data generation is combined with artificial intelligence approaches to realize rapid structure formation and property quantification of 3D graphene foams with mathematically controlled topologies, driven by molecular dynamics simulations. More than 4000 different foam structures are created, which feature diverse topologies that contain potential pathways for small molecules and auxetic structures with negative Poisson's ratio. Empowered by machine learning (ML) algorithms including graph neural networks, not only global properties such as elastic moduli, but also local behaviors such as atomic stress can be predicted and optimized based on their atomic structure, bypassing expensive atomistic simulations. The key findings of the research reported in this paper include a highthroughput virtual framework of generating diverse 3D graphene assemblies with mechanical performances quantification, and highly efficient methods of evaluating physical properties based on ML.
results: To generate the graphene metamaterials dataset, we start from a series of surface formulas that control the foam topologies mathematically. Six basis set functions in the form of trigonometric functions are superimposed to describe a family of TPMS-like surfaces. The coefficients of the superposition which can be denoted as a vector [*a*1, *a*2, *a*3, *a*4, *a*5, *a*6] vary from −1 to 1 to form diverse families of graphene foam. Each foam is within a periodic unit cell of 30 Å × 30 Å × 30 Å. Details about the selection of surface formulas are discussed in the Experimental Section (see Surface Formula of Graphene Foams). With the target topology, a high-throughput framework is developed to generate graphene foam structures following six steps: 1) random initialization, 2) triangularization to create triangular tessellation, 3) Voronoi patterning to create hexagonal tessellation, 4) relaxation using MD simulations, 5) screening based on relaxed structures, and 6) property measurement using non-equilibrium MD (NEMD) simulations. The similar procedure has been commonly utilized in the literature to create curved surfaces of graphene. Based on those two criteria, the final dataset after screening ends up with 4427 different graphene foams. With relaxed and screened foam structures, the mechanical behaviors are investigated under uniaxial tension and compression tests along the *z* direction. NEMD simulations are performed to compress or stretch the foam and relevant data including stress–strain response and atomic stress field are collected.
discussion: In this work, we generate more than 4000 graphene foams with diverse structures controlled by mathematical formulas using the high-throughput data generation technique. Mechanical performances including both global properties such as moduli and local behaviors including atomic stress are quantified and collected to train ML algorithms and DL models for fast predictions. Besides mechanical responses, other properties such as thermal and electric conductivities are also important and interesting to investigate given the fascinating electronic behaviors of 2D graphene sheets. The detailed calculation is left for future works, but the defect concentration shown by this work can already provide some insights as those properties are strongly affected by the structural defects. In terms of scaling law, it follows the principle known as "the smaller, the stronger" as a smaller unit cell has a higher density. Interestingly, the principle usually applies as there are less defects at the smaller scale. However, the generated graphene foam structures with smaller sizes in this work contain higher concentration of defects, which should lead to weaker foams. Nevertheless, the principle still holds as the density effect dominates.
conclusion: In summary, our work proposes a systematic and automatic framework of generating and evaluating 3D graphene assemblies with precisely controlled topologies, providing fundamental insights of mechanical performances for a wide range of structures. The parallel structure generation algorithm can be extended beyond the TPMS-like families to other curved shapes for different design purposes. The present research can offer guidance and reference for experimental studies on synthesized graphene foams by combing predictions from ML models with the scaling law. Both high-throughput virtual screening and in silico inverse design can be realized based on the proposed approach to reduce the cycle-time of structure optimization for different design purposes. Experimental methods such as the template-directed approach can possibly be utilized to synthesize the proposed designs to validate their respective performances.
